title: if testicular cancer patient, 5x poorer response to bleomycin chemotherapy
summary: if testicular cancer patient, 5x poorer response to bleomycin chemotherapy
detail: Testicular cancer patients may be treated with bleomycin, a cytotoxic drug that is essential component of chemotherapy regimens for this cancer, officially known as disseminated testicular germ-cell cancer (TC). rs1050565 is a SNP in the BLMH gene. This gene encodes a protein that can inactivate bleomycin. Based on a study of 300 TC patients treated with bleomycin, a testicular cancer patient with a rs1050565(G;G) genotype has an odds ratio of 4.97 (CI: 2.17 - 11.39) for TC-related death compared to (A;G) or (A;A) genotypes. The rs1050565(G;G) genotype also shows a higher prevalence of early relapses. Note that this gene affects bleomycin metabolism; the GG does not mean that a person is more likely to get testicular cancer.

detail: For the rs2011077 polymorphism, a significant increased risk of prostate cancer was found in men with the GG genotype (aOR = 6.260, 95% CI = 3.152–12.433, p < 0.001) or the GA genotype (aOR = 2.497, 95% CI = 1.717–3.630, p < 0.001) compared with the AA genotype. When GG, GA and AA genotypes were valued as 2, 1 and 0, respectively, the presence of the G allele was shown to increase the risk of prostate cancer with a gene dosage effect (aOR = 2.500, 95% CI = 1.871–3.339, p < 0.001). As for BPH, a significantly increased risk was found in the GG genotype (aOR = 3.033, 95% CI = 1.352–6.807, p = 0.007) and the GA genotype (aOR = 1.700, 95% CI = 1.077–2.683, p = 0.023) compared with the AA genotype. When GG, GA and AA genotypes were valued as 2, 1 and 0, respectively, the presence of the G allele was shown to increase the risk of BPH with a gene dosage effect (aOR = 1.724, 95% CI = 1.218–2.441, p = 0.002).
